      Evergrande
   =20
 =20
I am very far from an expert on the situation at giant indebted property co=
mpany China Evergrande Group, but I will say that two things that appear to=
 be true are:=09It owes  1.6 million apartments to people who have put down=
 cash deposits, and it may have trouble delivering them; and=09It owes bill=
ions of dollars in cash to people who have bought =E2=80=9Cwealth managemen=
t products=E2=80=9D (retail debt instruments), and it will have trouble pay=
ing them, to the point that it has offered to give them apartments instead =
of money.This strikes me as inefficient. If you have some apartments, you s=
hould deliver them to people to whom you owe apartments. Then you take the =
cash from those people and deliver it to the people to whom you owe cash? A=
gain I claim no expertise here and I am sure they have their reasons, but t=
hat=E2=80=99s how I would do it. I gather that the people with the wealth m=
anagement products do not want the apartments, but the people who have put =
down deposits for apartments do want the apartments.[1]It seems to me that =
what is interesting about Evergrande is not so much the magnitude of its  d=
ebt problems but their=C2=A0variety. Evergrande owes money to Chinese banks=
. It owes money to  foreign hedge funds, and foreign investors own its stoc=
k. It owes money to suppliers, and to Chinese retail investors in those wea=
lth management products. And it owes apartments to buyers. And the retail i=
nvestors who bought Evergrande wealth management products were often also E=
vergrande homeowners, because the products were sold at Evergrande building=
s:=E2=80=9CMy parents put the bulk of their savings, which is Rmb200,000 an=
d not a lot by Evergrande=E2=80=99s standard, into its [wealth management p=
roducts],=E2=80=9D said the daughter of one investor who asked to be identi=
fied by her surname Xu.=C2=A0She said an Evergrande financial adviser stati=
oned in an apartment tower built by the company in central China had persua=
ded her mother to invest. =E2=80=9CThey wouldn=E2=80=99t have trusted Everg=
rande=E2=80=99s wealth products had they not bought the developer=E2=80=99s=
 apartment,=E2=80=9D she said.In fact they were apparently advertised in th=
e elevators:"I bought from the property managers after seeing the ad in the=
 elevator, as I trusted Evergrande for being a Fortune Global 500 company,=
=E2=80=9D said the owner of an Evergrande property in the conglomerate's ho=
me province of Guangdong surnamed Du.And it also sold the wealth management=
 products to employees:When the troubled Chinese property giant Evergrande =
was starved for cash earlier this year, it turned to its own employees with=
 a strong-arm pitch: Those who wanted to keep their bonuses would have to g=
ive Evergrande a short-term loan.Some workers tapped their friends and fami=
ly for money to lend to the company. Others borrowed from the bank. Then, t=
his month, Evergrande suddenly stopped paying back the loans, which had bee=
n packaged as high-interest investments. =E2=80=A6The extent of the campaig=
n and how much money it might have raised were unclear. Employees were told=
 to each invest a certain amount of money in Evergrande Wealth products, an=
d that if they failed to do so, their performance pay and bonuses would be =
docked, employees told Anhui.These products were simultaneously (1) =E2=80=
=9Ccategorised as fixed-income products suitable for =E2=80=98conservative =
investors seeking steady returns=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D and (2) sold with 11% yi=
elds. Seems bad!When a big company runs out of money, the basic questions a=
re (1) who gets paid and who doesn=E2=80=99t and (2) should the government =
pay its debts for it? Those questions are interconnected. There is an ordin=
ary way to answer the first question, some waterfall of claim seniority.=C2=
=A0You look at the company=E2=80=99s capital structure and say =E2=80=9Cwel=
l these people have senior claims and will get paid back, and these people =
have junior claims and won=E2=80=99t, and these other people are somewhere =
in the middle and might get some recovery.=E2=80=9D And there are complex a=
nd subtle questions about the best way to preserve value in the business: P=
erhaps you have the legal right to stiff customers (perhaps their deposits =
aren=E2=80=99t particularly senior claims), but if you do that you=E2=80=99=
ll never get any more customers, so you treat them better than you are lega=
lly required to. And the managers of the business and the creditors and the=
 lawyers work together to figure out a plan that maximizes the recovery for=
 everyone.But if the ordinary=C2=A0process to answer the first question end=
s up with an answer like=C2=A0=E2=80=9Csympathetic ordinary people lose the=
ir life savings,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cpolitically connected people lose eve=
rything,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cthe banking system loses a lot of money and b=
ecomes undercapitalized,=E2=80=9D or for that matter =E2=80=9Chousing price=
s collapse,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0then that is a good reason for the government to =
step in. And if the government is stepping in, there is no particular reaso=
n to assume that the ordinary claims of seniority will apply. If the govern=
ment steps in to rescue small investors or the banking system or housing pr=
ices, that doesn=E2=80=99t necessarily mean it will also rescue foreign hed=
ge funds.=C2=A0Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Joe Weisenthal and Tracy Alloway did an =
 Odd Lots episode with analyst Travis Lundy about this, in which he gives h=
is best guess at a waterfall of repayment. =E2=80=9CI think that if you sta=
rt from the ranking of who ends up coming out well on this, if you had to a=
sk, this is the Communist Party of China who's the most important stakehold=
er in this,=E2=80=9D he says, and then goes through a list of claimants ord=
ered by, basically, how politically sympathetic they are. This seems like a=
 more reasonable analysis than, like, looking at the corporate structure an=
d legal document to see which claims are more senior.=C2=A0Much of the writ=
ing about Evergrande has been about =E2=80=9Cis this China=E2=80=99s  Lehma=
n moment?=E2=80=9D The main lesson of Lehman was that the collapse of a big=
 levered interconnected firm could cause serious economic damage, and since=
 Lehman, financial regulators in the U.S. and Europe have done a lot of wor=
k on reducing leverage and interconnection and damage.But another lesson, o=
ne that I think about a lot around here, is that the way to reduce systemic=
 risk and potential bailouts is for everyone to know how much risk they are=
 taking, for risks to come with clear warnings and accurate labels, and for=
 the risks to be taken by people who can handle them. If you must have big =
interconnected companies, it is good to know in advance whose claims are se=
nior and safe and who is taking a big gamble in the hope of a high return. =
It is fine for a company to fund itself by selling speculative investments =
to retail gamblers, and it is fine for a company to fund itself by selling =
safe-as-houses investments to retail retirement savers, but either way it i=
s important for people to know which one they=E2=80=99re buying. Much post-=
Lehman  financial  regulation is about this sort of labeling: The way to pr=
event after-the-fact government bailouts is by making sure that risk is bor=
ne by people who bear it knowingly and can afford to. When companies fail, =
people will lose money, and you want to be able to say to whoever loses mon=
ey, =E2=80=9Cwell, you knew what you were getting into.=E2=80=9DHere that s=
eems hard! Evergrande got its financing from absolutely everyone =E2=80=94 =
banks, investors, suppliers, customers, employees =E2=80=94=C2=A0and it see=
ms unlikely that they all=C2=A0knew what they were getting into. Its capita=
l structure branches out everywhere; there are all sorts of sympathetic peo=
ple who might lose money, and it is not obvious who should be rescued and w=
ho shouldn=E2=80=99t be. Do you give the apartments to the people who put t=
heir hard-earned money into apartment deposits, or the ones who put their h=
ard-earned money into wealth management products?=C2=A0Eventually either Ev=
ergrande will muddle through, or it will get a bailout, or it won=E2=80=99t=
 and people will lose money. But the long-term work of making sure this doe=
sn=E2=80=99t happen again is mostly about transparency, about allocating ri=
sks clearly in advance so you don=E2=80=99t have to sort them out in hindsi=
ght.

   =20
      Everything is securities fraud
   =20
 =20
=E2=80=9CEverything is securities fraud,=E2=80=9D  I like to say, and sexua=
l harassment is particularly securities fraud. If an executive at a public =
company sexually harasses colleagues, and the news gets out and the stock d=
rops, shareholders will sue the company, saying something like =E2=80=9Cyou=
 said that you had a code of ethics and policies against harassment, but yo=
u didn=E2=80=99t say that your executive was harassing people, so we were m=
isled into buying your stock, and when the truth came out we lost money.=E2=
=80=9D And then the company will settle and pay the shareholders something =
for the harassment. We have  talked about  this a few  times. Sexual harass=
ment, very much a core form of securities fraud.Usually the U.S. Securities=
 and Exchange Commission doesn=E2=80=99t bring harassment-as-securities-fra=
ud cases, though; that tends to be reserved for private plaintiffs=E2=80=99=
 lawyers working for shareholders. (I mean, working for a chunk of the sett=
lement.) But that is changing:Federal securities regulators have launched a=
 wide-ranging investigation into Activision Blizzard Inc., including how th=
e videogame-publishing giant handled employees=E2=80=99 allegations of sexu=
al misconduct and workplace discrimination, according to people familiar wi=
th the investigation and documents viewed by The Wall Street Journal. =E2=
=80=A6The agency is asking for documents including minutes from Activision =
board meetings since 2019, personnel files of six former employees and sepa=
ration agreements the company has reached this year with staffers, records =
show. The SEC is asking for Mr. Kotick=E2=80=99s communications with other =
senior executives regarding complaints of sexual harassment or discriminati=
on by Activision employees or contractors, the documents show.When I write =
about =E2=80=9Ceverything is securities fraud,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0mainly I am wr=
iting about plaintiffs=E2=80=99 lawyers. =E2=80=9CEverything is securities =
fraud=E2=80=9D is essentially a theory of the incentives facing plaintiffs=
=E2=80=99 lawyers, a way to transmute lots of problems at public companies =
into securities-fraud claims that are (1) easier to pursue and (2) more luc=
rative. If the stock drops when bad news comes out, it=E2=80=99s easy to br=
ing a class action on behalf of every shareholder who bought the stock befo=
re the news came out, and easy to calculate their damages (how much the sto=
ck dropped), which are large. Whereas just suing for sexual harassment is m=
ore difficult: Each case is unique, damages might be hard to prove and liti=
gating might be painful for the victims.It=E2=80=99s not obvious that the s=
ame analysis would apply to the SEC. There are actually government agencies=
 specifically tasked with investigating discrimination and harassment, and =
they are investigating Activision too:The California Department of Fair Emp=
loyment and Housing sued Activision in late July, alleging the company paid=
 women less than their male counterparts and provided them with fewer oppor=
tunities to advance. The agency also alleged that Activision ignored compla=
ints by female employees of sexual harassment, discrimination and retaliati=
on and maintained a =E2=80=9Cfrat-boy=E2=80=9D culture, primarily at its Bl=
izzard Entertainment unit. =E2=80=A6Separately, the Equal Employment Opport=
unity Commission has been examining allegations of gender-based harassment =
at Activision since at least May 2020, according to people familiar with th=
e investigation and documents viewed by the Journal.The company is in settl=
ement talks with the agency and could pay millions of dollars to resolve th=
e matter, according to those people.=C2=A0Still, everything is=C2=A0securit=
ies fraud. I  said the other day that the SEC is =E2=80=9Cthe U.S.=E2=80=99=
s most general-purpose regulatory agency=E2=80=9D: Securities disclosure to=
uches everything, from financial stability to climate change to Chinese-Ame=
rican relations to  presidential war powers. Everything bad that happens, a=
lso happens to shareholders, and the SEC is there to protect them.

   =20
      Analyst work/life balance
   =20
 =20
There are two conflicting theories of junior hiring on Wall Street. On the =
one hand, Ace Greenberg memorably sought to hire people =E2=80=9Cwith PSD d=
egrees=E2=80=9D: =E2=80=9CPoor, Smart, with a deep Desire to become rich.=
=E2=80=9D Wall Street is a place with lots of money, and you want junior pe=
ople who are very motivated by money so they will work hard; junior people =
who don=E2=80=99t already have money are more likely to be motivated by it.=
On the other hand, as I  said yesterday, Wall Street is essentially in the =
business of building deep personal relationships with giant piles of money,=
 and if you come from money you have better odds of doing that. If you grow=
 up rich you are more likely to be able to swap sailing anecdotes with a cl=
ient, or discover that you have neighboring estates=C2=A0in Bridgehampton, =
or =E2=80=94=C2=A0and this is particularly helpful =E2=80=94=C2=A0you might=
 be the client=E2=80=99s child. If your mother runs a large public company =
or a  state-owned enterprise=C2=A0or a multibillion-dollar family office, a=
nd you go to work for an investment bank, that investment bank is more like=
ly to win lucrative business from her, isn=E2=80=99t it? The bank certainly=
  thinks so.=C2=A0You don=E2=80=99t really have to choose, though; an inves=
tment bank hires lots of people each year, and the=C2=A0the solution here i=
s to build an analyst class with some smart driven people who are maybe a b=
it rough around the edges, and=C2=A0some smart rich people who are impressi=
ve polo players.Still there is some awkwardness in that everyone will start=
 at the firm in basically the same job (analyst), and they will be expected=
 to work hard, and they might have different reactions to that. The people =
that you hired because they were driven will say =E2=80=9Chuh, 100-hour wee=
ks, I=E2=80=99d better distinguish myself,=E2=80=9D and work 120 hours a we=
ek. The people that you hired for their golf handicaps and sprezzatura will=
 say =E2=80=9Coh I=E2=80=99m sorry I can=E2=80=99t work late tonight, I=E2=
=80=99m having dinner with my uncle at Sant Ambroeus,=E2=80=9D and their vi=
ce president will say =E2=80=9Cthat is unacceptable, you need to work on th=
e presentation for our client meeting tomorrow,=E2=80=9D and the analyst wi=
ll say =E2=80=9Cmy uncle runs a private equity firm=E2=80=9D and the VP wil=
l say =E2=80=9Cugh fine I guess.=E2=80=9D Multiple ways to the top, really,=
 but there is going to be some resentment on both sides.=C2=A0We talked thi=
s summer about Jamie Lee, the son of legendary JPMorgan Chase & Co. investm=
ent banker Jimmy Lee, who left banking because =E2=80=9Cthe opportunity cos=
t is simply too high to be sticking around in a job where you=E2=80=99re no=
t getting the treatment that you want,=E2=80=9D and whose father =E2=80=9Cu=
rged him to avoid the analyst programs=E2=80=9D: =E2=80=9CHe said, =E2=80=
=98Honestly, J, the way that I=E2=80=99ve seen that we work these kids, I=
=E2=80=99m not sure that I want that for you.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D The Ace Gre=
enberg theory is that you should hire people who really want the paycheck a=
nd would never dream of saying =E2=80=9Cthe opportunity cost is simply too =
high to be sticking around in a job where you=E2=80=99re not getting the tr=
eatment that you want.=E2=80=9D But if Jimmy Lee=E2=80=99s son applies for =
a job, what are you gonna do, say no?Anyway  this story made me laugh:The u=
nauthorized Goldman slide deck that went viral early this year, laying out =
the frustrations of 13 junior investment bankers, set off the closest thing=
 modern Wall Street has ever had to a proletariat uprising from within. Sur=
prisingly, a newbie at the center of the project has deep roots in the busi=
ness.Joey Coslet was a first-year analyst at Goldman when he played a key r=
ole in assembling the presentation that ricocheted around the industry and =
beyond, according to people with knowledge of the situation. His father, Jo=
nathan Coslet, had been chief investment officer and is now vice chairman o=
f TPG, a major Goldman client.The deck -- innocuously titled =E2=80=9CWorki=
ng Conditions Survey=E2=80=9D -- struck a nerve by vividly describing the t=
oll of 120-hour work weeks. That prompted months of debate over whether it=
=E2=80=99s time to ease up on the industry=E2=80=99s newcomers. Goldman Sac=
hs Group Inc. renewed its pledge to give junior bankers Saturdays off and b=
oosted their base pay to $110,000. There=E2=80=99s now virtually no major f=
irm left offering less than six figures. =E2=80=A6Coslet, 23, interned at G=
oldman and TPG before graduating from the University of Pennsylvania=E2=80=
=99s Wharton School and returning to the bank. The firm placed him on a cov=
eted team catering to the investment-banking needs of the red-hot tech sect=
or.Investment-banking analyst programs are notoriously intense, but also se=
en as so beneficial to a successful Wall Street career that it=E2=80=99s no=
t uncommon for the offspring of well-connected financiers to start out as j=
unior bankers at top firms.

   =20
      NFT Stuff
   =20
 =20
I write about non-fungible tokens a lot around here because they are the cr=
ypto world=E2=80=99s leading source=C2=A0of (1) cool financial experimentat=
ion=C2=A0and (2) laughable scams. I like both of those things! In the laugh=
able scams category, Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Justina Lee and Madeline Campbell =
have a story about  how many NFTs are worthless:As co-founder of a website =
tracking the spectrum of digital collectibles, Gauthier Zuppinger has seen =
it all. Drawings of toads, apes, abstract blobs, octopuses on heads, fire-b=
reathing devils, and more.=C2=A0Since last month, as non-fungible tokens su=
pplanted meme stocks and minor coins in speculative imaginations, his websi=
te has added 169 collections -- more than the prior 12 months combined. For=
 anyone convinced they possess an investing edge to become the industry=E2=
=80=99s Warren Buffett, the chief operating officer of Nonfungible.com has =
a word of warning.=E2=80=9CMaybe 90% of collections minted today are totall=
y useless and meaningless,=E2=80=9D Zuppinger said from Paris.=C2=A0Last we=
ek=E2=80=99s $24 million Sotheby=E2=80=99s auction of ape tokens, the $180 =
million Doge meme and other surreal superlatives from the summer of NFTs ha=
ve all painted a pixelated picture of easy money. But the market is in fact=
 a vast sprawl of varying investing outcomes, data compiled by Bloomberg sh=
ow. =E2=80=A6One of the most prevalent investing outcomes: Getting stuck wi=
th something nobody else wants. In the 90 days through Monday, roughly 1.9 =
million assets were sold on the largest marketplace OpenSea. But about thre=
e quarters never saw another transaction.=C2=A0Well, but maybe all those pe=
ople bought all those assets because they felt a deep personal and aestheti=
c connection to them? Most=C2=A0art=C2=A0isn=E2=80=99t resold repeatedly wi=
thin 90 days; most people buy art to hang it on their walls and look at it =
and enjoy it. Perhaps people are doing the same with the pixelated images t=
hey buy? I mean, fine, no, it seems very unlikely.But maybe? Here is a funn=
y New York Times newsletter by Jay Caspian Kang in which he=C2=A0says that =
the =E2=80=9Ccrude and somewhat blandly cartoonish=E2=80=9D virtual toad im=
age that he bought =E2=80=9Cbring[s] me quite a bit of joy that I don=E2=80=
=99t fully understand,=E2=80=9D though he also says he =E2=80=9Csold my toa=
d before the publication of this newsletter.=E2=80=9D If you like your pixe=
l things and don=E2=80=99t want to sell them, more power to you. If you are=
 buying your pixel things to get rich in a meaningless speculative game the=
n, uh, not=C2=A0everyone=C2=A0is going to get rich in the meaningless specu=
lative game? In the long run, people get rich by=C2=A0creating economic val=
ue. If some people are just trading nonsense among themselves, it is hard t=
o see how they can all get rich.Also someone recently sent me a link to thi=
s NFT project of 100 blocks of =E2=80=9CNegative Space=E2=80=9D:Negative Sp=
ace #001 is a 420 x 420 block of negative space and is part of limited seri=
es of 100 negative spaces by 27B/6. The use of negative space is a key elem=
ent of composition where effective communication is the objective. Clients =
rarely understand the importance of negative space and will often request i=
t be filled with larger type or superfluous elements.These negative spaces =
are not. They are just blank images, which you can =E2=80=9Chave=E2=80=9D o=
n the blockchain. Whatever.=C2=A0Obviously most NFTs are, like, you buy som=
e tenuous rights to an ugly digital image, but I am curious what percentage=
 are like =E2=80=9Cyou buy tenuous rights to literally, explicitly nothing.=
=E2=80=9D It seems like there are a lot of them, though they may=C2=A0be ov=
errepresented in my email inbox.In the cool financial experimentation categ=
ory, here is a proposal for something called =E2=80=9CMartingale Shares,=E2=
=80=9D or =E2=80=9CMortys,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0from Dave White at Paradigm. It is=
 loosely speaking a way to sell fractional interests in NFTs, which is hot =
right now; we talked a  couple of  times recently about a craze for fractio=
nal shares of an NFT of a=C2=A0picture of a dog. But I do not think that th=
is is an=C2=A0essentially=C2=A0pixelated-image concept. Here is how I would=
 describe it:=09The owner of an asset puts the asset in a =E2=80=9Cvault.=
=E2=80=9D (As a traditional financial engineer, I would have said a =E2=80=
=9Cbox,=E2=80=9D but same idea.)=09The vault sells shares representing a po=
rtion of the ownership of the asset. Say there are 100 shares; 50=C2=A0go t=
o buyers, while the asset owner keeps 50.=09=E2=80=9CEvery night at midnigh=
t, the Morty protocol flips one coin per vault=E2=80=9D and moves one share=
. With 50% probability, the asset owner loses a share to the buyers; with 5=
0% probability, the buyers lose one share to the asset owner.=09Eventually =
either=C2=A0the asset owner gets back to 100 shares and gets her asset back=
 free and clear (and keeps the cash from the initial sale in step 2);=C2=A0=
or=C2=A0the asset owner falls to 0 shares and hands her asset over to the b=
uyers (and was effectively paid 50% of the value of the asset for the whole=
 asset);=C2=A0or=C2=A0it bounces around between 1 and 99 forever.=09The sha=
res represent not a=C2=A0particular=C2=A0asset but a=C2=A0class=C2=A0of ass=
et: every NFT of a particular series, or every NFT of that series with some=
 specified attribute, whatever is specified when the vault is set up. The a=
sset owner can swap any NFT of the class in for the particular one in the v=
ault; if it looks like she is going to lose her NFT, and she is emotionally=
 attached to it, she can go out and buy another one in the class to substit=
ute in. Or in financial terms, there is a cheapest-to-deliver option: Ordin=
arily, the=C2=A0shares represent a (possible) entitlement to the cheapest d=
eliverable NFT in the class.=09Similarly, because the shares represent a cl=
ass, they are=C2=A0fungible. If two asset owners each with an NFT of the sa=
me class put their NFTs in a vault, =E2=80=9CMortys=E2=80=9D of those two N=
FTs are fungible with each other. If=C2=A0either=C2=A0of those vaults falls=
 to zero shares, the buyers will get (a proportional interest in) one of th=
e NFTs. (Some protocol might auction off the NFT and distribute the proceed=
s to the Morty holders, or they could own it jointly, or whatever.)I am not=
 sure how, or if, all the details would work, but the basic idea is that ow=
ners of individual assets=C2=A0could=C2=A0raise money by selling into a mar=
ket of fungible claims on a=C2=A0class=C2=A0of assets, and that those claim=
s are=C2=A0probabilistic; instead of getting like a 20% equity share in the=
 asset you get effectively a 20% probability=C2=A0of owning the asset outri=
ght. The goal is to build markets of liquid fungible tokens to finance owne=
rship of non-fungible tokens.Does that sound silly? In writing about this W=
hite uses what I can only hope is a fictional NFT series called the =E2=80=
=9CAwful Hot Ocelots=E2=80=9D:Alice=E2=80=99s vault and Mortys are of the W=
izard Hat Ocelot class, meaning they can be created only with Wizard Hat Oc=
elots. All Mortys of a given class are fungible. =E2=80=A6 Alice could have=
 chosen a class with stricter requirements, such as Ocelots with both a Wiz=
ard Hat and a Scarf. Or, she could have chosen a class with looser requirem=
ents, such as the floor Ocelot class, which could be created with any Ocelo=
t at all. More complex classes are possible, such as a class of Mortys that=
 can be minted with either one Ocelot or two Armadillos, NFTs from an entir=
ely different project.And you=E2=80=99d feel like a bit of an idiot actuall=
y setting up any of those things. But the obvious trade here is not =E2=80=
=9Cyou can put any Awful Hot Ocelot with a Wizard Hat in the vault=E2=80=9D=
; the obvious trade here is =E2=80=9Cyou can put any investment-grade corpo=
rate bond with at least five=C2=A0years=E2=80=99 remaining maturity in the =
vault.=E2=80=9D The deep trade is =E2=80=9Cyou can put any three-bedroom ho=
use in the Minneapolis metro area appraised at at least $550,000 in the vau=
lt.=E2=80=9D (The deep trade is =E2=80=9Cyou can create classes of somewhat=
 fungible instruments to finance arbitrary non-fungible assets.=E2=80=9D)=
=C2=A0The important thing here is applying crypto-y principles to=C2=A0trad=
ing actual things, to trading claims on real-world cash flows or assets.Tha=
t is why NFTs are interesting: They are a sandbox for building financial to=
ols that can represent the real world in the crypto system. Early on, thoug=
h, no one is going to use these tools for real-world applications. They=E2=
=80=99re going to use them for trivial digital pictures of toads and stuff;=
 if the tools work, someone will find a way to apply them to real economic =
activity. I think that is interesting, and there is at least some chance th=
at the tools and protocols and mechanisms will turn out to be valuable. I d=
on=E2=80=99t know why that would be good for the pictures of toads though.

5px;">Evergrande</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am very far from an expert on the si=
tuation at giant indebted property company China Evergrande Group, but I wi=
ll say that two things that appear to be true are:</p><ol style=3D"list-sty=
le-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"marg=
in-bottom: 5px;">It owes <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=
/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyMS8wOS8xOS9idXNp=
bmVzcy9jaGluYS1ldmVyZ3JhbmRlLWRlYnQtcHJvdGVzdHMuaHRtbA/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB9a5a3b3f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;"> 1.6 million apartments</a> to people who have put down cash deposits,=
 and it may have trouble delivering them; and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bo=
ttom: 5px;">It owes billions of dollars in cash to people who have bought =
=E2=80=9Cwealth management products=E2=80=9D (retail debt instruments), and=
 it will have trouble paying them, to the point that it has offered to <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMTkvZXZlcmdyYW5kZ=
S1zdGFydHMtcmVwYXlpbmctaW52ZXN0b3JzLXdpdGgtZGlzY291bnRlZC1wcm9wZXJ0aWVz/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2050930b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">give them apartments instead of money</a>.</li></ol><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This strikes me as inefficient. If you have som=
e apartments, you should deliver them to people to whom you owe apartments.=
 Then you take the cash from those people and deliver it to the people to w=
hom you owe cash? Again I claim no expertise here and I am sure they have t=
heir reasons, but that=E2=80=99s how I would do it. I gather that the peopl=
e with the wealth management products do not want the apartments, but the p=
eople who have put down deposits for apartments do want the apartments.[1]<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It seems to me that what is interesting abo=
ut Evergrande is not so much the magnitude of its <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMTgvZXZlcmdyYW5kZS1tb21lbnQtb2YtdHJ1dG=
gtYXJyaXZlcy13aXRoLWJvbmQtcGF5bWVudC1kZWFkbGluZXM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
a37a53e0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 debt problems</a> but their=C2=A0<em>variety</em>. Evergrande owes money t=
o Chinese banks. It owes money to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDktMjAvc2FiYS1yZWR3b29kLWFyZS1hbW9uZy1jcmVkaXQtZnVuZHMtYn=
V5aW5nLWV2ZXJncmFuZGUtYm9uZHM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5cac9ea8" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> foreign hedge fun=
ds</a>, and foreign investors own its stock. It owes money to suppliers, an=
d to Chinese retail investors in those wealth management products. And it o=
wes apartments to buyers. And the retail investors who bought Evergrande we=
alth management products were often also Evergrande homeowners, because the=
 products were <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/251=
04125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMGIwM2Q0ZGUtMTY2Mi00ZDMwLW=
JjZmQtYzliYWUyNGZhOWNj/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe7bad0a4" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">sold at Evergrande building=
s</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CMy parents put =
the bulk of their savings, which is Rmb200,000 and not a lot by Evergrande=
=E2=80=99s standard, into its [wealth management products],=E2=80=9D said t=
he daughter of one investor who asked to be identified by her surname Xu.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">She said an Evergrande financial adv=
iser stationed in an apartment tower built by the company in central China =
had persuaded her mother to invest. =E2=80=9CThey wouldn=E2=80=99t have tru=
sted Evergrande=E2=80=99s wealth products had they not bought the developer=
=E2=80=99s apartment,=E2=80=9D she said.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">In fact they were apparently <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cucmV1dGVycy5jb20vYnVza=
W5lc3MvZmluYW5jZS93aXRoLWd1Y2NpLWJhZ3MtZHlzb24tYXBwbGlhbmNlcy1ldmVyZ3JhbmRl=
LXdvb2VkLXJldGFpbC1pbnZlc3RvcnMtMjAyMS0wOS0yMS8/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0f=
dbdc0f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000=
000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bot=
tom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">ad=
vertised in the elevators</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
"I bought from the property managers after seeing the ad in the elevator, a=
s I trusted Evergrande for being a Fortune Global 500 company,=E2=80=9D sai=
d the owner of an Evergrande property in the conglomerate's home province o=
f Guangdong surnamed Du.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And i=
t also sold the wealth management products <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMj=
AyMS8wOS8xOS9idXNpbmVzcy9jaGluYS1ldmVyZ3JhbmRlLWRlYnQtcHJvdGVzdHMuaHRtbA/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC9a5a3b3f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">to employees</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">When the troubled Chinese property giant Evergrande was starved f=
or cash earlier this year, it turned to its own employees with a strong-arm=
 pitch: Those who wanted to keep their bonuses would have to give Evergrand=
e a short-term loan.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some workers tapped th=
eir friends and family for money to lend to the company. Others borrowed fr=
om the bank. Then, this month, Evergrande suddenly stopped paying back the =
loans, which had been packaged as high-interest investments. =E2=80=A6</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The extent of the campaign and how much money i=
t might have raised were unclear. Employees were told to each invest a cert=
ain amount of money in Evergrande Wealth products, and that if they failed =
to do so, their performance pay and bonuses would be docked, employees told=
 Anhui.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These products were <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cucmV1dGVycy5jb20vYnVzaW5lc3MvZmluYW5jZS93aXRoLWd1Y2NpLWJhZ3MtZHl=
zb24tYXBwbGlhbmNlcy1ldmVyZ3JhbmRlLXdvb2VkLXJldGFpbC1pbnZlc3RvcnMtMjAyMS0wOS=
0yMS8/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC0fdbdc0f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">simultaneously</a> (1) =E2=80=9Ccategorise=
d as fixed-income products suitable for =E2=80=98conservative investors see=
king steady returns=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D and (2) sold with 11% yields. Seems b=
ad!</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When a big company runs out of money, t=
he basic questions are (1) who gets paid and who doesn=E2=80=99t and (2) sh=
ould the government pay its debts for it? Those questions are interconnecte=
d. There is an ordinary way to answer the first question, some waterfall of=
 claim seniority.=C2=A0You look at the company=E2=80=99s capital structure =
and say =E2=80=9Cwell these people have senior claims and will get paid bac=
k, and these people have junior claims and won=E2=80=99t, and these other p=
eople are somewhere in the middle and might get some recovery.=E2=80=9D And=
 there are complex and subtle questions about the best way to preserve valu=
e in the business: Perhaps you have the legal right to stiff customers (per=
haps their deposits aren=E2=80=99t particularly senior claims), but if you =
do that you=E2=80=99ll never get any more customers, so you treat them bett=
er than you are legally required to. And the managers of the business and t=
he creditors and the lawyers work together to figure out a plan that maximi=
zes the recovery for everyone.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But if the o=
rdinary=C2=A0process to answer the first question ends up with an answer li=
ke=C2=A0=E2=80=9Csympathetic ordinary people lose their life savings,=E2=80=
=9D or =E2=80=9Cpolitically connected people lose everything,=E2=80=9D or =
=E2=80=9Cthe banking system loses a lot of money and becomes undercapitaliz=
ed,=E2=80=9D or for that matter =E2=80=9Chousing prices collapse,=E2=80=9D=
=C2=A0then that is a good reason for the government to step in. And if the =
government is stepping in, there is no particular reason to assume that the=
 ordinary claims of seniority will apply. If the government steps in to res=
cue small investors or the banking system or housing prices, that doesn=E2=
=80=99t necessarily mean it will also rescue foreign hedge funds.=C2=A0</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Joe Weisenthal and Tracy A=
lloway did an <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2510=
4125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDk=
tMjEvdHJhbnNjcmlwdC11bmRlcnN0YW5kaW5nLWV2ZXJncmFuZGUtdGhlLXRyb3VibGVkLWNoaW=
5lc2UtcmVhbC1lc3RhdGUtZ2lhbnQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3e3a4161" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Odd Lots episode<=
/a> with analyst Travis Lundy about this, in which he gives his best guess =
at a waterfall of repayment. =E2=80=9CI think that if you start from the ra=
nking of who ends up coming out well on this, if you had to ask, this is th=
e Communist Party of China who's the most important stakeholder in this,=E2=
=80=9D he says, and then goes through a list of claimants ordered by, basic=
ally, how politically sympathetic they are. This seems like a more reasonab=
le analysis than, like, looking at the corporate structure and legal docume=
nt to see which claims are more senior.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">Much of the writing about Evergrande has been about =E2=80=9Cis this Chi=
na=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104=
125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEt=
MDktMjAvY2hpbmEtcy1ldmVyZ3JhbmRlLW1vbWVudC1pcy1sb29raW5nLW1vcmUtbHRjbS10aGF=
uLW1pbnNreQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9a20d955" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" items=
cope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importan=
t; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-st=
yle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Lehman moment</a>?=E2=80=9D The main =
lesson of Lehman was that the collapse of a big levered interconnected firm=
 could cause serious economic damage, and since Lehman, financial regulator=
s in the U.S. and Europe have done a lot of work on reducing leverage and i=
nterconnection and damage.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But another less=
on, one that I think about a lot around here, is that the way to reduce sys=
temic risk and potential bailouts is for everyone to know how much risk the=
y are taking, for risks to come with clear warnings and accurate labels, an=
d for the risks to be taken by people who can handle them. If you must have=
 big interconnected companies, it is good to know in advance whose claims a=
re senior and safe and who is taking a big gamble in the hope of a high ret=
urn. It is fine for a company to fund itself by selling speculative investm=
ents to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.2=
56842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL25ld3NsZXR0ZXJzLzIwMjEtMDYtM=
DIvbW9uZXktc3R1ZmYtYW1jLWJyaW5ncy1vdXQtdGhlLXBvcGNvcm4/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB1d2e2e25" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">retail gamblers</a>, and it is fine for a company to fund itself by se=
lling safe-as-houses investments to retail retirement savers, but either wa=
y it is important for people to know which one they=E2=80=99re buying. <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTgtMDUtMjUvZXVyb3BlY=
W4tYmFua3MtZ2V0LWNyZWF0aXZlLXdpdGgtY2FwaXRhbC1yZWxpZWY/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB1a013bdb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">Much</a> post-Lehman <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.co=
m/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGl=
jbGVzLzIwMTktMDItMTMvc2FudGFuZGVyLWRpZG4tdC1wYXktaXRzLW5vbi1kZWJ0/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cBa65fc080" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;"> financial</a> <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMTctMDYtMDgvYmFpbC1pbnMtc29jaWFsLW5vcm1zLWFuZC1jb3dz/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cB47e971e9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> regulation</a> is about this sort of labeling: The way to=
 prevent after-the-fact government bailouts is by making sure that risk is =
borne by people who bear it knowingly and can afford to. When companies fai=
l, people will lose money, and you want to be able to say to whoever loses =
money, =E2=80=9Cwell, you knew what you were getting into.=E2=80=9D</p><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here that seems hard! Evergrande got its financing=
 from absolutely everyone =E2=80=94 banks, investors, suppliers, customers,=
 employees =E2=80=94=C2=A0and it seems unlikely that they all=C2=A0knew wha=
t they were getting into. Its capital structure branches out everywhere; th=
ere are all sorts of sympathetic people who might lose money, and it is not=
 obvious who should be rescued and who shouldn=E2=80=99t be. Do you give th=
e apartments to the people who put their hard-earned money into apartment d=
eposits, or the ones who put their hard-earned money into wealth management=
 products?=C2=A0Eventually either Evergrande will muddle through, or it wil=
l get a bailout, or it won=E2=80=99t and people will lose money. But the lo=
ng-term work of making sure this doesn=E2=80=99t happen again is mostly abo=
ut transparency, about allocating risks clearly in advance so you don=E2=80=
=99t have to sort them out in hindsight.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-spon=
sored-content" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0">

 border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> I like to say</a>, a=

omething for the harassment. We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=

 much a core form of securities fraud.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Usua=

usiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9zZ=

x 0;">Federal securities regulators have launched a wide-ranging investigat=
ion into Activision Blizzard Inc., including how the videogame-publishing g=
iant handled employees=E2=80=99 allegations of sexual misconduct and workpl=
ace discrimination, according to people familiar with the investigation and=
 documents viewed by The Wall Street Journal. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">The agency is asking for documents including minutes from Acti=
vision board meetings since 2019, personnel files of six former employees a=
nd separation agreements the company has reached this year with staffers, r=
ecords show. The SEC is asking for Mr. Kotick=E2=80=99s communications with=
 other senior executives regarding complaints of sexual harassment or discr=
imination by Activision employees or contractors, the documents show.</p></=
blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When I write about =E2=80=9Ceveryth=
ing is securities fraud,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<em>mainly </em>I am writing about p=
laintiffs=E2=80=99 lawyers. =E2=80=9CEverything is securities fraud=E2=80=
=9D is essentially a theory of the incentives facing plaintiffs=E2=80=99 la=
wyers, a way to transmute lots of problems at public companies into securit=
ies-fraud claims that are (1) easier to pursue and (2) more lucrative. If t=
he stock drops when bad news comes out, it=E2=80=99s easy to bring a class =
action on behalf of every shareholder who bought the stock before the news =
came out, and easy to calculate their damages (how much the stock dropped),=
 which are large. Whereas just suing for sexual harassment is more difficul=
t: Each case is unique, damages might be hard to prove and litigating might=
 be painful for the victims.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s n=
ot obvious that the same analysis would apply to the SEC. There are actuall=
y government agencies specifically tasked with investigating discrimination=
 and harassment, and they are investigating Activision too:</p><blockquote>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The California Department of Fair Employment a=
nd Housing sued Activision in late July, alleging the company paid women le=
ss than their male counterparts and provided them with fewer opportunities =
to advance. The agency also alleged that Activision ignored complaints by f=
emale employees of sexual harassment, discrimination and retaliation and ma=
intained a =E2=80=9Cfrat-boy=E2=80=9D culture, primarily at its Blizzard En=
tertainment unit. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Separately, the=
 Equal Employment Opportunity Commission has been examining allegations of =
gender-based harassment at Activision since at least May 2020, according to=
 people familiar with the investigation and documents viewed by the Journal=
.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The company is in settlement talks with t=
he agency and could pay millions of dollars to resolve the matter, accordin=
g to those people.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still=
, everything <em>is=C2=A0</em>securities fraud. I <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256842/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=

r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Poor, Smart</a>, with a dee=
p Desire to become rich.=E2=80=9D Wall Street is a place with lots of money=
, and you want junior people who are very motivated by money so they will w=
ork hard; junior people who don=E2=80=99t already have money are more likel=
y to be motivated by it.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand,=
 as I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25104125.256=

olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =

 #ccc;"> said yesterday</a>, Wall Street is essentially in the business of =

come from money you have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=

=C2=A0in Bridgehampton, or =E2=80=94=C2=A0and this is particularly helpful =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0you might be the client=E2=80=99s child. If your mother runs=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You don=E2=80=99t really have to choose, thoug=
h; an investment bank hires lots of people each year, and the=C2=A0the solu=
tion here is to build an analyst class with some smart driven people who ar=
e maybe a bit rough around the edges, and=C2=A0some smart rich people who a=
re impressive polo players.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still there is =
some awkwardness in that everyone will start at the firm in basically the s=
ame job (analyst), and they will be expected to work hard, and they might h=
ave different reactions to that. The people that you hired because they wer=

cle at Sant Ambroeus,=E2=80=9D and their vice president will say =E2=80=9Ct=
hat is unacceptable, you need to work on the presentation for our client me=
eting tomorrow,=E2=80=9D and the analyst will say =E2=80=9Cmy uncle runs a =
private equity firm=E2=80=9D and the VP will say =E2=80=9Cugh fine I guess.=
=E2=80=9D Multiple ways to the top, really, but there is going to be some r=

emscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=

of a dog. But I do not think that this is an=C2=A0<em>essentially=C2=A0</em=
>pixelated-image concept. Here is how I would describe it:</p><ol style=3D"=
list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The owner of an asset puts the asset in a =E2=80=
=9Cvault.=E2=80=9D (As a traditional financial engineer, I would have said =
a =E2=80=9Cbox,=E2=80=9D but same idea.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom:=
 5px;">The vault sells shares representing a portion of the ownership of th=
e asset. Say there are 100 shares; 50=C2=A0go to buyers, while the asset ow=
ner keeps 50.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=E2=80=9CEvery night=
 at midnight, the Morty protocol flips one coin per vault=E2=80=9D and move=
s one share. With 50% probability, the asset owner loses a share to the buy=
ers; with 50% probability, the buyers lose one share to the asset owner.</l=
i>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Eventually either=C2=A0the asset own=
er gets back to 100 shares and gets her asset back free and clear (and keep=
s the cash from the initial sale in step 2);=C2=A0<em>or=C2=A0</em>the asse=
t owner falls to 0 shares and hands her asset over to the buyers (and was e=
ffectively paid 50% of the value of the asset for the whole asset);=C2=A0<e=
m>or=C2=A0</em>it bounces around between 1 and 99 forever.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The shares represent not a=C2=A0<em>particular=C2=
=A0</em>asset but a=C2=A0<em>class=C2=A0</em>of asset: every NFT of a parti=
cular series, or every NFT of that series with some specified attribute, wh=
atever is specified when the vault is set up. The asset owner can swap any =
NFT of the class in for the particular one in the vault; if it looks like s=
he is going to lose her NFT, and she is emotionally attached to it, she can=
 go out and buy another one in the class to substitute in. Or in financial =
terms, there is a cheapest-to-deliver option: Ordinarily, the=C2=A0shares r=
epresent a (possible) entitlement to the cheapest deliverable NFT in the cl=
ass.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Similarly, because the shares=
 represent a class, they are=C2=A0<em>fungible</em>. If two asset owners ea=
ch with an NFT of the same class put their NFTs in a vault, =E2=80=9CMortys=
=E2=80=9D of those two NFTs are fungible with each other. If=C2=A0<em>eithe=
r=C2=A0</em>of those vaults falls to zero shares, the buyers will get (a pr=
oportional interest in) one of the NFTs. (Some protocol might auction off t=
he NFT and distribute the proceeds to the Morty holders, or they could own =
it jointly, or whatever.)</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am not su=
re how, or if, all the details would work, but the basic idea is that owner=
s of individual assets=C2=A0could=C2=A0raise money by selling into a market=
 of fungible claims on a=C2=A0<em>class=C2=A0</em>of assets, and that those=
 claims are=C2=A0<em>probabilistic</em>; instead of getting like a 20% equi=
ty share in the asset you get effectively a 20% <em>probability=C2=A0</em>o=
f owning the asset outright. The goal is to build markets of liquid fungibl=
e tokens to finance ownership of non-fungible tokens.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">Does that sound silly? In writing about this White uses what I c=
an only hope is a fictional NFT series called the =E2=80=9CAwful Hot Ocelot=
s=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Alice=E2=80=99s vau=
lt and Mortys are of the Wizard Hat Ocelot class, meaning they can be creat=
ed only with Wizard Hat Ocelots. All Mortys of a given class are fungible. =
=E2=80=A6 Alice could have chosen a class with stricter requirements, such =
as Ocelots with both a Wizard Hat and a Scarf. Or, she could have chosen a =
class with looser requirements, such as the floor Ocelot class, which could=
 be created with any Ocelot at all. More complex classes are possible, such=
 as a class of Mortys that can be minted with either one Ocelot or two Arma=
dillos, NFTs from an entirely different project.</p></blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And you=E2=80=99d feel like a bit of an idiot actually=
 setting up any of those things. But the obvious trade here is not =E2=80=
=9Cyou can put any Awful Hot Ocelot with a Wizard Hat in the vault=E2=80=9D=
; the obvious trade here is =E2=80=9Cyou can put any investment-grade corpo=
rate bond with at least five=C2=A0years=E2=80=99 remaining maturity in the =
vault.=E2=80=9D The deep trade is =E2=80=9Cyou can put any three-bedroom ho=
use in the Minneapolis metro area appraised at at least $550,000 in the vau=
lt.=E2=80=9D (The deep trade is =E2=80=9Cyou can create classes of somewhat=
 fungible instruments to finance arbitrary <em>non-fungible assets</em>.=E2=
=80=9D)=C2=A0The important thing here is applying crypto-y principles to=C2=
=A0<em>trading actual things</em>, to trading claims on real-world cash flo=
ws or assets.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is why NFTs are interest=
ing: They are a sandbox for building financial tools that can represent the=
 real world in the crypto system. Early on, though, no one is going to use =
these tools for real-world applications. They=E2=80=99re going to use them =
for trivial digital pictures of toads and stuff; if the tools work, someone=
 will find a way to apply them to real economic activity. I think that is i=
nteresting, and there is at least some chance that the tools and protocols =
and mechanisms will turn out to be valuable. I don=E2=80=99t know why that =
would be good for the pictures of toads though.</p><table class=3D"header" =
